column2: A wired workstation has no network connectivity, and the Ethernet port has no link light. What should you check first?
column3: Start with the evidence closest to the device.
column4: Check the physical connection: cable seating, cable condition, and the switch port or wall jack.
column5:
Why

No link light points to a physical-layer problem before it points to IP, gateway, DNS, or application settings. Reseating or swapping the cable and checking the connected port can quickly confirm whether the device has an actual network link.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Network PhysicalLayer Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user moved a desktop, and now the wired network connection is down. The link light is off. What is the most useful next test?
column3: Use a simple substitution test before changing software settings.
column4: Try a known-good Ethernet cable and, if needed, a known-good jack or switch port.
column5:
Why

After a move, the most likely change is physical: a loose patch cable, damaged cable, bad wall jack, or wrong switch port. A known-good substitution narrows the fault without introducing unnecessary configuration changes.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network PhysicalLayer Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A technician pings 127.0.0.1 while troubleshooting no connectivity. What does a successful reply show?
column4: The local TCP/IP stack is responding on the computer.
column5:
Why

The loopback test stays inside the host. A successful reply does not prove the network cable, wireless link, gateway, DNS, or Internet path works; it only supports that the local protocol stack can respond.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Network Ping Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A loopback ping succeeds. What should pinging the computer's own assigned IP address help verify next?
column4: Whether the host can respond through its configured network interface and IP configuration.
column5:
Why

Pinging the local assigned address moves one step beyond loopback. It helps confirm the host's network stack, local address, and adapter configuration before testing the gateway or remote destinations.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network Ping Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: What does a successful ping to the default gateway prove during a no-network troubleshooting path?
column4: The computer can reach the local network's router or gateway.
column5:
Why

The default gateway is the boundary between the local subnet and other networks. If it responds, the local link, local IP settings, and path to the router are more likely working, so the next question is whether traffic can go beyond that router.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Gateway Network Ping Source::Messer-v170

column2: A workstation can ping its default gateway, but it cannot ping a known remote IP address. What does that result suggest?
column3: The local boundary responds, but the farther destination does not.
column4: The failure is likely beyond the local gateway or on the upstream path.
column5:
Why

Gateway success makes a bad local cable or local IP configuration less likely. The failed remote IP test shifts the investigation past the local router instead of keeping the focus on the workstation alone.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Gateway Network Ping Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A Windows laptop reports limited connectivity and has a 169.254.x.x address. What is the likely network issue?
column4: The device did not receive a usable DHCP address and assigned itself a local automatic address.
column5:
Why

An APIPA-style address is useful troubleshooting evidence. It usually means the device is not successfully receiving DHCP configuration, so the technician should investigate DHCP reachability, wireless association, VLAN, cabling, or local network access instead of treating the Internet as the first suspect.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield APIPA DHCP Network Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A computer has a normal-looking DHCP address but still cannot reach websites. What is the best next network test?
column3: Do not stop at the address; test the path.
column4: Ping the default gateway, then a known remote IP address, to isolate whether the failure is local or beyond the router.
column5:
Why

A valid address only proves the host received configuration. Gateway and remote IP tests show where connectivity stops, which is more useful than immediately changing DNS, browser, or application settings.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic DHCP Gateway Network Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Wi-Fi drops mainly in a break room when appliances are in use. What likely wireless cause should you investigate first?
column4: Radio interference affecting the wireless signal.
column5:
Why

Intermittent wireless problems that correlate with a location or equipment use often point to interference. The technician should gather timing and location details before replacing client hardware or assuming the access point has failed.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Interference Network Wireless Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user's Wi-Fi works near the access point but drops at the far end of the office. What should you check first?
column4: Check wireless signal strength and coverage in that area.
column5:
Why

Distance and obstacles can weaken a Wi-Fi signal enough to cause roaming, disconnects, or slow speeds. Measuring or comparing signal strength in the problem area is a focused test before changing unrelated client settings.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network Signal Wireless Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Wi-Fi became unreliable after a manual access point channel change. What should the technician verify?
column3: Review the most recent wireless configuration change.
column4: Verify that the wireless channel or frequency configuration is appropriate for the environment.
column5:
Why

Incorrect or congested channel selection can create wireless instability even when the access point is functioning. This card tests the configuration clue, not a full wireless design exercise.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Channel Network Wireless Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Wi-Fi is inconsistent in a room with many reflective surfaces, even though the access point is nearby. What wireless effect should be considered?
column4: Signal bounce or multipath interference.
column5:
Why

Wireless signals can reflect and arrive out of phase, causing unstable service in specific locations. The important troubleshooting clue is that the symptom is location-specific despite apparent proximity to the access point.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Multipath Network Wireless Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Several users in the same corner of a building report weak Wi-Fi, while nearby areas work normally. What likely infrastructure issue should you investigate?
column4: Access point placement or coverage for that area.
column5:
Why

Multiple users affected in the same physical area points away from one bad laptop. It is more useful to check coverage and AP placement before replacing client adapters.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network Wireless Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user says, "The network is slow." What should the technician do before changing network equipment?
column4: Confirm the symptom with a specific application, destination, time, or network test.
column5:
Why

"Slow network" can mean an application issue, wireless issue, local device problem, server problem, or provider issue. Gathering measurable evidence keeps the troubleshooting path systematic and avoids guessing.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Network Performance Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Users report poor Internet performance, and you need a quick throughput comparison against expected service. What test is useful?
column4: Run a speed test from an appropriate client or network location.
column5:
Why

A speed test gives a measurable data point for download, upload, and sometimes latency. It does not diagnose every cause by itself, but it helps compare the observed performance with expected service or historical baselines.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network Performance Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Performance is good on the local LAN but becomes slow after one network hop. What does that test result suggest?
column4: Focus on that hop or the path beyond it, such as congestion, errors, filtering, or an upstream link problem.
column5:
Why

Comparing performance across segments helps isolate where the slowdown begins. The answer is not to replace the user's workstation; the evidence points to a specific boundary in the network path.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network Performance Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Simple ping and speed tests do not explain an ongoing slow-network complaint. What evidence-gathering method can show what traffic is actually occurring?
column3: Move from summary tests to traffic evidence.
column4: Use a packet capture at an appropriate point in the path.
column5:
Why

Packet captures can reveal retransmissions, failed sessions, excessive chatter, or unexpected traffic. For A+ scope, the learning target is when capture evidence becomes useful, not exact capture syntax.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network PacketCapture Performance Source::Messer-v170

column2: Voice and video calls are choppy even when the connection does not fully drop. What network condition is a likely cause?
column4: Jitter, or inconsistent delay between packets.
column5:
Why

Real-time traffic is sensitive to uneven packet arrival. A connection can have enough bandwidth for a speed test and still sound bad if delay varies significantly during the call.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Jitter Network VoIP Source::Messer-v170

column2: Several users report poor VoIP call quality. What broad network condition should you verify early?
column3: Start with the path real-time media depends on.
column4: Verify Internet connection performance, especially speed and latency.
column5:
Why

VoIP problems often surface when the WAN or Internet path cannot support real-time traffic consistently. Checking speed and latency early helps separate a provider/path problem from one bad phone or headset.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Network Performance VoIP Source::Messer-v170

column2: Internet speed looks normal, but multiple VoIP phones at one site still have poor call quality. What local network area should you check next?
column4: Check the local network equipment and path used by the phones, such as the router, switch, cabling, and configuration.
column5:
Why

Once the broad Internet path looks healthy, the investigation should move to the local path shared by the affected phones. Multiple devices at one site make one bad handset less likely.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network Router VoIP Source::Messer-v170

column2: VoIP quality problems persist, and basic speed and ping tests do not explain them. What can provide more detailed evidence?
column4: A packet capture or network performance analysis at the affected path.
column5:
Why

Voice problems can depend on timing, loss, retransmissions, or traffic handling that simple tests miss. Capture or performance evidence helps escalation teams avoid guessing.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network PacketCapture VoIP Source::Messer-v170

column2: A switch port repeatedly goes up and down for the same workstation. What should you check first?
column3: Start with the simplest part of the physical path.
column4: Check or replace the cable and verify the wiring path.
column5:
Why

Port flapping often starts with a physical link problem. Cable and wiring checks are low-risk and help avoid replacing switch hardware before proving the link path is stable.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Network PhysicalLayer PortFlapping Source::Messer-v170

column2: A workstation's switch port is flapping, and a known-good cable did not fix it. What is a useful next isolation test?
column4: Move the connection to another known-good switch port and compare the result.
column5:
Why

Changing one variable at a time helps isolate whether the issue follows the device/cable path or stays with the switch interface. This is more useful than guessing which component to replace.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network PortFlapping Switch Source::Messer-v170

column2: A port-flapping problem follows the same workstation and patch cable to a different switch port. What does that suggest?
column4: The issue likely follows the endpoint or its cable path, not the original switch port.
column5:
Why

When the symptom moves with the device or cable, the original switch interface is less likely to be the root cause. The next checks should focus on the endpoint NIC, patch cable, dock, wall run, or local connection path.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network PortFlapping Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: A port-flapping problem stops when the workstation is moved to another switch port. What does that result suggest?
column4: The original switch interface or its configuration is more likely involved.
column5:
Why

If the endpoint and cable path work on a different port, the evidence shifts back to the original switch interface. The technician should document the test and escalate or repair according to local network procedures.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Network PortFlapping Switch Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user reports that cloud apps respond slowly, but local file shares are fast. What should you measure next?
column4: Measure latency along the path to the remote service or beyond the local network.
column5:
Why

Fast local resources make a general workstation problem less likely. Latency tests help identify whether delay begins after traffic leaves the local network.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Latency Network Performance Source::Messer-v170

column2: An escalation team needs precise timing evidence for a latency complaint. What method can provide high-detail packet timing?
column4: Collect packet captures from the relevant sides of the path for timing comparison.
column5:
Why

Packet captures can show when packets leave and arrive at different points. That timing evidence is useful when ordinary user reports or single ping tests are not precise enough for escalation.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Latency Network PacketCapture Source::Messer-v170

column2: A wireless survey shows low signal-to-noise ratio in the area where users drop connections. What does that suggest?
column4: The useful wireless signal is not sufficiently stronger than the surrounding noise.
column5:
Why

Signal strength alone is not the whole story. A client can be close to an access point and still perform poorly if the noise level is high enough to make the signal difficult to distinguish.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Network SNR Wireless Source::Messer-v170

column2: A user can reach the network, but one service repeatedly reports authentication failures after a long session. What simple step should you try first?
column4: Have the user sign out and sign back in, or otherwise refresh the session according to the service's normal process.
column5:
Why

Authentication symptoms can come from expired sessions, cached credentials, or account state rather than a broken network path. A session refresh is a low-risk first step when the symptom is limited to authentication.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Authentication Network Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: An authentication problem happens in the background, and the user never sees a clear error message. What can help reveal the failed communication?
column4: A packet capture or network trace from the affected system.
column5:
Why

Some authentication failures are hidden inside background service traffic. A capture can show whether the system reaches the authentication service and whether communication fails before, during, or after the authentication attempt.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Authentication Network PacketCapture Source::Messer-v170

column2: Several users report Internet drops at the same time, but local network resources stay available. What should you determine first?
column4: Determine the scope by testing internal resources and external destinations from more than one affected client.
column5:
Why

If local resources remain reachable while external destinations fail, the problem may be on the Internet edge or provider path. Scope evidence is stronger than a single user's report.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield ISP Network Scenario Source::Messer-v170

column2: Internet connectivity is intermittent, and pings show occasional loss to a remote destination. What path test can show where traffic stops or slows?
column4: Run a traceroute or equivalent route-tracing test to the remote destination.
column5:
Why

Route tracing helps identify whether loss or delay appears near the local network, at the provider edge, or farther away. It is evidence for escalation, not a guarantee that the failing hop is the root cause by itself.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic ISP Network Traceroute Source::Messer-v170

column2: Internal tests are normal, but external pings and route tests show Internet path failures. What is the appropriate escalation?
column4: Contact the ISP or provider with the gathered test evidence and account information.
column5:
Why

Provider escalation is stronger when the technician can show internal resources work and external paths fail. Account details, timestamps, affected scope, and test output help the provider act on the ticket.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic HighYield Escalation ISP Network Source::Messer-v170

column2: An ISP outage is confirmed. What document helps set expectations for provider response or uptime commitments?
column4: The service level agreement.
column5:
Why

An SLA defines service expectations such as availability, response, or support terms. It helps the technician communicate realistic next steps instead of promising a repair time without a contractual basis.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic ISP Network SLA Source::Messer-v170

column2: Internet drops are intermittent, and the provider asks for evidence. What performance data should you collect over time?
column4: Document timestamps with ping results, traceroute results, and speed test results when symptoms occur.
column5:
Why

Intermittent problems are difficult to prove with one clean test. Time-stamped evidence helps correlate user impact with measurable loss, latency, route changes, or throughput drops.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic ISP Network Performance Source::Messer-v170

column2: You are opening an ISP ticket for intermittent Internet connectivity. What should you document before escalation?
column4: Affected scope, timestamps, internal versus external test results, ping or traceroute evidence, speed test results, and account/contact information.
column5:
Why

Clear documentation helps the provider reproduce and triage the issue. It also shows that local checks were performed before escalation.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.56; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Documentation ISP Network Source::Messer-v170

column2: You are escalating a no-connectivity issue to the network team. What concise evidence should you include?
column4: Link-light status, cable or port tests, IP configuration, gateway test result, remote test result, affected scope, and recent changes.
column5:
Why

Escalation is more useful when it describes what has already been isolated. This prevents the next technician from repeating basic checks and helps them focus on the unresolved part of the path.
column9: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.55; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 5.5
column10: A+::220-1201::5.5 A+::220-1201::Domain5-Troubleshooting A+::220-1201::TroubleshootingNetworks Basic Documentation Escalation Network Source::Messer-v170
